title: Lecture 5 Corporate Bonds
body:
Asset Pricing with Corporate Bonds
Lecture 5
 Lecture Outline

 Corporate Bond Stylized Facts
 Characterizing Corporate Bonds
 Corporate Bond Returns
 Credit Risk and Credit Spreads
 Decomposing Bond Returns – Credit & Interest Rate Risk
 Anomalies in the Bond Market
     The CAPM for Corporate Bonds
     Bond Anomalies




                                        FINS 3640
 Stylized Fact #1: Size of the Corporate Bond Market




   •   Size of the U.S. corporate bond market as of 2021 was 10 Trillion U.S. Dollars.
   •   Comparatively, the size of the U.S. Equity market was roughly 40 Trillion U.S. Dollars.
   •   U.S. Corporate bond issuance, per year, has increased from 285 Billion in 1996 to over 1 Trillion from 2012 onward.

                                               Source: Data from SIFMA Research

                                                        FINS 3640
 Stylized Fact #2: The Bond Market is relatively Illiquid




    •   The amount of trading activity in the U.S. stock market is an order of magnitude higher than the bond market.
    •   Bond trading volume was 26 Bn compared to 25 Tr for stocks in 2019.
    •   Bonds trade exclusively “Over-the-Counter” (OTC) across a network of broker-dealers.
    •   Stocks primarily trade on a “limit order book” (LOB).
                                         Source: Data from SIFMA Research & World Bank

                                                        FINS 3640
 Stylized Fact #3: Industry Profile of Issuers (in the U.S)




   • Almost a quarter (24.35%) of the bond market is comprised of banks, insurers and other financial firms.
   • This is followed by bonds issued by Energy firms (11%) and Industrial firms (8.1%).


                                                 FINS 3640
 Stylized Fact #4: Empirical Default Rates are Low




•   Average default rate for all corporate bonds from 1997 to 2021 is 0.28%, i.e. of the 24,920 eligible bonds in the sample,
    only 70 of them defaulted (had a S&P Rating of C or D). We learn about ratings later on in the lecture.
•   Also note that bond agencies’ ratings (S&P, Moody’s, etc…) lag market based information such as the bond credit spread.
•   Later on, we will use changes in a credit spread index to predict the changes in the average rating for the aggregate bond
    market.
                                                         FINS 3640
     Characterizing Corporate Bonds
    Unlike stocks, bonds are highly heterogenous and differ substantially in the cross-section

•   Maturity:                                                                                      •   Bond Ratings:
-   Short-term notes (maturities up to 5-years).
                                                                                                   -   Investment grade bonds (rated BBB- and upward for S&P) or Baa3 and upward for
-   Medium-term notes (maturities between 5 and 12 years)                                              Moody’s.

-   Long-term bonds (maturities over 12-years)                                                     -   Speculative grade (junk) bonds rated BB+ / Ba1 and below.
-   Median bond maturity in the U.S. market is 5-Years.                                            -   In industry, these markets are highly segmented. Traders will operate on different desks,
                                                                                                       i.e., the “junk bond desk”. Most pension funds and asset managers cannot trade
•   Coupon:                                                                                            speculative grade debt.
-   Floating Rate Note (FRN): coupon adjusts with a market related reference rate.                 •   Par Value/ Face Value:
-   Fixed rate coupon: bond pays fixed coupon throughout its tenor.
                                                                                                   -   The principal amount or principal value of the bond.
-   Zero-coupon – no coupon payments.
                                                                                                   -   The amount the bond issuer will pay when the bond matures.
-   Payment-in-Kind (PIK): bond coupon may be in the firm of cash or additional securities.
                                                                                                   -   Bond par value is almost always $1,000 and is very rarely $10,000.
-   Most often payments are made semi-annually.
                                                                                                   •   Seniority structure / subordination:
•   Bond options
                                                                                                   -   Most bonds are classified as “Senior Unsecured”, meaning the bonds are issued without
-   Over 80% of corporate bonds are embedded with a call option available to the issuer. If            any collateral guaranteeing them. Senior secured bonds are backed with collateral.
    the price exceeds a certain threshold, the bond can be called back (i.e. the debt is paid –
    off at the call strike price). Call options benefit the bond issuer.                           -   Junior and subordinated debtholders are only paid after the senior (secured and
-   Very few bonds are embedded with a put option (<0.10%). A put option benefits the bond             unsecured) debtholders are paid.
    holder. If the bond price breaches a threshold from above, the debt can be “put”, i.e., sold
    back to the issuer at the strike price.
-   Few bonds give the option to the holder to convert their debt to an equivalent equity
    value – these are “convertible” bonds.




                                                                                          FINS 3640
 Calculating Corporate Bond Returns
                                                                                                               𝐸𝐸
                                                                                          𝐸𝐸     𝑃𝑃 +𝐷𝐷𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡
Computing the monthly return on a corporate bond is slightly more complex than stocks: 𝑟𝑟𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡 = 𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡𝐸𝐸     −1
                                                                                                   𝑃𝑃           𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡−1

• For a given bond i, the monthly total return at the end of month t is computed as,

                                                                       𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵
                                                       𝐵𝐵𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜
                                                                    𝑃𝑃𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡     + 𝐴𝐴𝐼𝐼𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡 + 𝐶𝐶𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡
                                                    𝑟𝑟𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡     =         𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵
                                                                                                        −1
                                                                       𝑃𝑃𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡−1   + 𝐴𝐴𝐼𝐼𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡−1
            𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵
• where, 𝑃𝑃𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡     is the “clean price” of the bond, 𝐴𝐴𝐼𝐼𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡 is the bond accrued interest and 𝐶𝐶𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡 is the bond coupon payment (if any)
  during month t.
• The clean price of a bond does not include any accrued interest. This is the “price” of the bond that is quoted by dealers
  and presented in prominent financial newspapers (i.e., by Bloomberg or the FT).
                                                                                                           𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵
• The “Dirty”, “Full” or “Invoice” price is the clean price of the bond plus any accrued interest, i.e.,𝑃𝑃𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡     + 𝐴𝐴𝐼𝐼𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡 .
• If you trade a corporate bond before or after it’s coupon date, you must account for accrued interest in the return
  computation
• The accrued interest accounts for the time that the “old” owner held the bond. They must be compensated for this.
• Accrued interest depends on the characteristics of the bond, including it’s “day-count” convention and frequency of coupon
  payment.
• Roughly 95% of all corporate bonds in the U.S. pay “semi-annual” coupons with a day-count frequency of “30/360” and “par
  value” of $1,000.
                                                                       FINS 3640
 Calculating Corporate Bond Returns – Definition - Part 1
Clean Price: The price of the bond without accrued interest. Also known as the quoted priced. Determined by market forces
and quoted by broker-dealers, who post price quotes of the bonds that they are willing to trade at. In this case, the yield can
be inferred from the quoted price. In the U.S., corporate bond market prices are quoted as a percentage of par. For example, a
bond quote of 99.505 means the bond is trading at 99.505% of par value (which is almost always $1,000), implying the bonds
clean price is $995.05.


Trade Date: The date that the bond trade is physically executed by the bond trader who works for the broker-dealer. The price
paid is always the price of the asset (bond) on the trade date. For example, if you are purchasing a bond for 90.56 on the 30th
January 2023, this is the price you will pay for the bond (excluding accrued interest).


Settlement Date: Over 90% of all bond trades settle “t+2” (Source: TRACE data), meaning, that after 2 business days of the
trade date, the asset (bonds) are legally transferred to the purchaser's account. In other words, this is the date that the bond is
legally exchanged between the buyer and seller. Bond accrued interest only stops accruing on the trade date.


Dirty Price: Also known as the invoice price. This is the total price that the purchaser of a bond pays the broker-dealer on the
trade execution date. This is the sum of the clean price of the bond and the accrued interest (which is computed to include the
number of days between the trade and settlement date). The exact number of days is, of course, known on the trade date and
is normally 2.


                                                           FINS 3640
 Calculating Corporate Bond Returns – Definition - Part 2


Accrued Interest: The amount of interest that has accrued to the holder of record since the last coupon payment up until the
                                                  𝐶𝐶      𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷 𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠 𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙 𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃
trade settlement date. Computed as: 𝐴𝐴𝐼𝐼𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡 = 𝑖𝑖 ×                                           , where 𝐶𝐶𝑖𝑖 is the annual coupon, and 𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝑄𝑄, is the
                                               𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹 𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷 𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶
interest frequency.


Day Count Convention: When purchasing a bond between coupon dates, there are different conventions for computing the
legal amount of bond accrued interest. The most common day-count convention is the “30/360”, method which assumes there
are exactly 30 days in any given calendar month. Other conventions include “ACT/ACT” and “ACT/360”.


Interest Frequency: The frequency (𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹) at which bonds pay-out coupons.


Total Bond Return: The total return of a bond, defined as the current clean price plus any coupon paid out in month t plus
accrued interest, divided by the sum of the clean price and accrued interest from the prior month.




                                                                     FINS 3640
 Application 1: Calculating Bond Returns between Coupon Dates
• Your investment firm, Dandy Associates, is researching a large investment in a BBB S&P rated bond
  issued by Frenchie Inc., a company that specializes in French cosmetics. The bond pays coupons
  semi-annually at a rate of 10% and has a 30/360 day-count convention. The proposed purchase date
  would be 31st May 2017. The bond’s coupon dates are 1st January and 1st July every year. You may
  assume the settlement date is the same as the trade date. The quoted price of the bond (clean price)
  from your dealer (Morgan Stanley) was 99.70 of par.

Questions:

1. Explain the difference between the clean and dirty price of a bond. Give definitions of any
   appropriate terms used in your answer.
2. Intuitively, why must we account for accrued interests when computing the dirty (invoice) price of a
   bond?
3. Compute the amount of accrued interest on the bond on 31th May 2017 (150 days have passed
   since 1st January using the 30/360 day-count convention). What is the dirty price of the bond?
4. If the price (% of par value) and accrued interest of the bond was 95.20 and $3.33 respectively on
   the 30th April 2017, what was the monthly return on the bond in May?

                                                FINS 3640
  Application 1: Calculating Bond Returns between Coupon Dates – Solution
Questions:

1. Explain the difference between the clean and dirty price of a bond. Give definitions of any appropriate terms used in your
   answer.

Clean Price: The clean price is the price of a coupon bond not including accrued interest payments. The clean price is typically
the quoted price on financial news sites. This price does not include any interest accrued between the scheduled coupon
payments for the bond. The clean price is the quote price of the bond from a broker-dealer. The clean price is not computed
by the investor, it is a market driven price that is “offered” to you by a broker-dealer. Bond yield can be inferred from the
clean price and associated bond coupon schedule. The clean price is the price of the bond on the trade execution date.

Dirty Price: A dirty bond price is a bond pricing quote (clean price) plus any bond accrued interest based on the coupon rate.
Bonds almost always trade between coupon dates, meaning accrued interest must be compute to derive the dirty (or invoice
price) of the bond.

Accrued Interest: Accrued interest on a bond is the amount of interest earned on a bond from the most recent coupon
payment to the current date but not yet received by the bondholder. It is calculated as the product of the bond's coupon
rate, face value, and the fraction of time elapsed since the last coupon payment. Bond accrued interest accrues from the
exact date of the last coupon payment up until the trade settlement date, which is generally 2-business days after the trade
execution date.

                                                        FINS 3640
  Application 1: Calculating Bond Returns between Coupon Dates – Solution
Questions:

2. Intuitively, why must one factor in accrued interest when computing the dirty (invoice) price of a bond?

                                                             𝐶𝐶𝑖𝑖      𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷 𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠𝑠 𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙𝑙 𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃𝑃
The amount of accrued interest is equal to: 𝐴𝐴𝐼𝐼𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡 =            ×                                        .
                                                          𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹   𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷 𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏𝑏 𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶


This amount has been “earned” by the seller of the bond. Thus, they must be compensated for owning the bond for this between
coupon dates (they will not actually receive the physical coupon). The accrued interest is the additional amount the purchaser will
pay the seller.

3. Compute the amount of accrued interest on the bond on 31st May 2017 (150 days have passed since 1st January using the
30/360 day-count convention). What is the dirty price of the bond?

The value of the semi-annual coupon is 1000 x 10%/2 = 50 (we are using % of par value, so it is 5). Accrued interest is then 5 x
150/180 = 4.1667. The dirty price of the bond is 99.70 + 4.1667 = 103.8667

4. If the price (% of par) and accrued interest of the bond was 95.20 and $3.33 respectively on the 30th April 2017, what was the
monthly return on the bond?
The monthly return on the bond will be:

                                      𝐵𝐵𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜     99.70 + 4.1667 + 0
                                   𝑟𝑟𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡     =                                − 1 = 0.0541 × 100 = 𝟓𝟓. 𝟒𝟒𝟒𝟒%
                                                     95.20 + 3.33

                                                                              FINS 3640
 Application 2: Calculating Monthly Bond Returns – Excel

• See Application 1 in the Lecture 5 Excel Sheet.
• We examine the CCC+ S&P rated subordinated debt issue from AMC (one of the meme stocks post COVID-19)
  from 2004-11 to 2012-08 (bond CUSIP number is “001669AQ”).
• A bond CUSIP is akin to a stock ticker, it (uniquely) identifies the bond issue (a stock ticker does NOT uniquely
  identify the stock, many stocks have the same ticker).
• The Excel spreadsheet contains the Date, the Clean Price of the bond (pre-computed by the data provider),
  the Cash-Flow (coupon of the bond, if any at the end of month t) , various accrued interest computations
  (ACT/ACT, ACT/360 and 30/360), bond Dirty Price and total corporate bond returns.
• The remaining columns calculate the number of actual days after a coupon payment using the actual number
  of days and the number of days using the “30/360” convention.
• We calculate bond returns at the end of every month.
     Bond Credit Spreads (Yield Spreads)
•    Bond credit spread (CS) indicates the difference between the yields of two bonds with the same maturity or duration but
     different credit ratings or credit risk profile.
•    The higher the spread is the riskier the corporate bond (higher default risk).
•    Examining bond credit spreads is crucial – it allows investors to breakdown the macroeconomic and microeconomic risks
     that corporate bonds are exposed too.

•    The most generic formula for bond credit spread is:

      CS = Bond Yield – Benchmark/Governement Yield

The Benchmark Yield
• The benchmark yield is almost always a government bond yield which has been interpolated to have a similar maturity as
   the reference bond.
• The benchmark yield (from now the government bond yield) captures macroeconomic factors which are systematic. These
   include the expected rate of inflation, and the expected real risk-free rate.
• These factors effect all bonds and their changes are primarily reflected in changes in risk-free government bonds (the
   benchmark set of yields).

The Credit Spread
• Captures microeconomic factors that are unique to each bond.
• These include the credit risk of the issuer, liquidity risk and the tax status of the bond.
• However, the majority of the credit spread is due to the default risk of the issuing firm.
                                                              FINS 3640
 Building Blocks of Bond Yields




     • The Benchmark is often referred to as the Risk-Free rate of return and is decomposed into the expected real rate of
       return and the expected inflation rate in the economy.
     • The credit spread is the referred to as the “Risk Premium” over the “Risk-Free” rate of return. This risk premium
       compensates investors for the credit and liquidity risks and in some cases the tax impact of holding a given
       corporate bond.
     • Our focus will be the “Credit Risk” component of the credit spread.
     Calculating Bond Credit Spreads
•    Bond credit spreads can be computed in several ways and are presented in basis points (bps).

(1) The G-Spread
•    The G-spread is simply the bond yield minus the actual or interpolated government bond yield (the benchmark). In practice, we linearly interpolate government bond
     yields such that they match the corporate bond maturity at the end of any month t.
•    The spread is higher for bond issuers with a greater degree of credit risk.

                                                                                                                           𝐺𝐺
                                                                                    𝐶𝐶𝑆𝑆𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 = 𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 − 𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 ,

                                                                                                                            𝐺𝐺
where 𝐶𝐶𝑆𝑆𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡 is the bond G-spread, 𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 is the yield of bond i at the end of month t of maturity T, and 𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 is the maturity matched government bond yield

(2) The Z-Spread
•    The G-Spread uses the same discount rate for each cash-flow (i.e. the yield of the bond).
•    An alternative is to compute the credit spread over a government zero-coupon bond spot curve, where each bond coupon payment is discounted at a different rate.
•    The bond Z-Spread is given as:

                                                                                                                                            ⃛
                                                                                   𝐶𝐶                    𝐶𝐶                    𝐶𝐶                  𝐶𝐶+𝐹𝐹𝐹𝐹
                                                              𝑃𝑃0𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵𝐵 =                 1   +                 2   +                 3                      𝑇𝑇   ,
                                                                              1+𝑟𝑟0,1 +𝑠𝑠           1+𝑟𝑟0,2 +𝑠𝑠           1+𝑟𝑟0,3 +𝑠𝑠           1+𝑟𝑟0,𝑇𝑇 +𝑠𝑠


where the benchmark spot rates are 𝑟𝑟0,1 , … , 𝑟𝑟0,𝑇𝑇 are from the government bond yield curve. s is the Z-spread per period and is constant.
• Z (the credit spread) is the yield spread which has to be added to a benchmark yield curve to discount a security's payments to match its market price.
• The Z-spread is most widely used in practice because it uses the entire yield curve to value the cash flows of the bond and provides a more accurate credit spread than the G-
  spread, which simply measures the difference in the yield of a bond and the maturity matched government bond.

(3) The Option Adjusted Spread (OAS)
• If the Z-spread adjusted for the value of a bonds embedded option (if any).
• Recall, over 80% of all corporate debt in the U.S. contains an embedded call option. However, empirically, the adjustment has a very minor effect.




                                                                                                    FINS 3640
 Bond Yields and Credit Spreads in the Time-Series




 •   Average yield and credit spread (Option adjusted) for the U.S. Corporate Bond market was 6.09% and 2.75% respectively over the
     sample period 1996-12 to 2021-06.
 •   Yields and credit spreads are strongly counter-cyclical, meaning they move in opposite directions to the economy (are negatively
     correlated to GDP growth and consumption).
 •   Spike upward in times of market distress (2001/02, Dotcom Bubble, 2007/08 Financial Crisis, 2015/16 European Debt Crisis, 2020
     COVID-19 Pandemic).
                                                               FINS 3640
     Application 3: Calculating Bond G-Spreads
•    You are a bond portfolio manager, working for Bondi Capital. A client has approached you to recommend a bond for her
     portfolio. The client has a preference for bonds with high credit risk. All interest rate risk is hedged, i.e. the client will
     only cares about the bond’s spread over the benchmark. You source 3 potential bonds, with their yields and maturities
     below (as of March 31st 2022):

•    Bond 1: Yield = 10% and Maturity = 10-Years
•    Bond 2: Yield = 13% and Maturity = 5.75-Years
•    Bond 3: Yield = 14.75% and Maturity = 3.20-years

•    You collect the relevant yield curve rates (as of March 31st) which are as follows:

                                 𝐺𝐺
•    2-year Yield = 2.28% (𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,2 )
                                 𝐺𝐺
•    5-Year Yield = 2.42% (𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,5 )
                                 𝐺𝐺
•    7-year Yield = 2.40% (𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,7 )
                                 𝐺𝐺
•    10-year Yield= 2.32% (𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,10  )

•    Compute the G-spread of each bond and give a recommendation to the client.
•    Assume exactly 365 days in a year (i.e. 1-year = 365 days).




                                                               FINS 3640
 Application 3: Calculating Bond G-Spreads - Solution
•   Given that taxation and liquidity concerns are non-existent and our client want maximum exposure to credit risk, we should select the bond that has the
    highest credit spread (assuming all else equal).

Bond 1: The credit spread is simply 10% - 2.32% = 7.68%

Bond 2: This bond has a maturity of T=5.75-Years, meaning we must linearly interpolate the yield curve between the 5-year and 7-year U.S. Government bond yield:

                                                                               𝐺𝐺          𝐺𝐺
                                                                𝐺𝐺
                                                                         𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,7
                                                                             𝐺𝐺
                                                                                   − 𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,5
                                                          𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,𝑇𝑇 = 𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,5 +             𝑇𝑇 − 𝑇𝑇5
                                                                               𝑇𝑇7 − 𝑇𝑇5
                                                             2.40% − 2.42%
                                                   = 2.42% +                  × 5.75 − 5 = 𝟐𝟐. 𝟒𝟒𝟒𝟒𝟒𝟒𝟒𝟒𝟒
                                                                 (7 − 5)

•   The credit spread is then equal to: 13% - 2.4125% = 10.5875%

Bond 3: This bond has a maturity of 3.20-Years, meaning we must linearly interpolate the yield curve between the 2-year and 5-year U.S. Government bond yield:

                                                                                𝐺𝐺          𝐺𝐺
                                                                𝐺𝐺
                                                                          𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,5
                                                                             𝐺𝐺
                                                                                    − 𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,2
                                                          𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,𝑇𝑇 = 𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑌𝑀𝑀0,2 +              𝑇𝑇 − 𝑇𝑇2
                                                                                𝑇𝑇5 − 𝑇𝑇2
                                                              2.42% − 2.28%
                                                    = 2.28% +                   × 3.20 − 2 = 𝟐𝟐. 𝟑𝟑𝟑𝟑𝟑𝟑𝟑
                                                                  (5 − 2)

•   The credit spread is then equal to: 14.50% - 2.336% = 12.414%


Recommendation, holding all else equal, and assuming the client cares only for credit risk, you would recommend Bond Number 2 with the highest credit spread
(12.58%).

 Application 4: Calculating Bond G-Spreads – Excel

• See Application 2 in the Lecture 5 Excel Sheet.
• We examine the CCC+ S&P rated subordinated debt issue from AMC (one of the meme stocks post COVID-19)
  from 2004-11 to 2012-08 (bond CUSIP number is “001669AQ”).
• The Excel spreadsheet contains the Date, the OAS Z-Spread of the bond (pre-computed by the data provider),
  the maturity of the bond in each month (tmt – “time-to-maturity”), the bond’s yield to maturity (YTM) and
  “key rate” U.S. Constant Government Bond Yields for 1, 2, 5, 7, 10, 20 and 30-years.
• The column “Interpolated Yield Curve” computes the G-Spread of the AMC bond at the end of each month t
  by linearly interpolating the U.S. Government yield curve, given the maturity of the bond (tmt).
• The G-Spread is then simply the YTM of the bond minus the interpolated yield curve for each month t.
• Note that in this case, the G-Spread and the OAS Z-Spread are extremely similar, with a correlation of 0.99993,
  i.e. the embedded optionality of the bond does not matter much in this case. Nor does discounting the bonds
  coupons with the spot curve.

     Credit Ratings - Overview

•    What are Bond Credit Ratings?

•    Bond credit ratings are representations of the creditworthiness of corporate or government bonds. The ratings are
     published by credit rating agencies and provide evaluations of a bond issuer’s financial strength and capacity to repay
     the bond’s principal and interest according to the contract.

•    The three private “independent” rating agencies – S&P, Moody’s, and Fitch – control almost 95% of the market share of
     the bond rating business. Each rating agency uses its own grading system.

•    The independence of these companies is questionable, and all of them contributed to the financial meltdown in
     2007/08 by assigning very high grade ratings to bonds (and packages of bonds) that were extremely risky.

•    Bond ratings depend on many factors including the strength of the bond issuer’s balance sheet, issuer fundamentals
     and cash-flows.

•    (A) Why would an investor buy a D rated bond as opposed to a AAA rated bond?
•    (B) What is the benefit to a company who has been assigned a AAA rating by S&P?
•    (C) What is the relationship between credit spreads and bond credit ratings?
 Credit Ratings – S&P, Moody’s and Fitch




 •   Bonds rated AAA or Aaa, are said to be of the “highest quality”, with minimal to non-existent credit risk. This
     rating is extremely rare – only Microsoft and Johnson & Johnson are currently AAA rated in the U.S.
 •   Bonds rated below BBB- or Baa3 are known as speculative grade or “junk” bonds. The credit risk and associated
     risk of default of these bonds are much higher than investment grade bonds.
     Credit Ratings – Issuer vs. Issue Ratings
•    Bond rating agencies typically provide both a “issuer” credit rating and a bond specific “issue” rating.
•    The issuer credit rating applies to the company as a whole, whilst the issuer rating will sometimes differ across the
     firms outstanding bonds.
•    The issuer credit rating is generally applied to the firms senior unsecured debt.
•    Issue credit ratings are adjusted starting from the issuer credit rating and take into account the subordination level of
     the bond.
•    The ratings adjustment process that the agencies use to adjust the issuer rating for each individual bond issue is
     known as “notching”.
•    For example, if Microsoft issues a junior subordinated bond issue, the rating for this issue will almost certainly be
     notched downward from the firms senior unsecured rating of AAA.

Question #1: What is the difference between an issuer rating and an issue rating?
The issuer rating is an assessment of the firms overall credit worthiness, generally reflected as the senior unsecured rating, whereas the
issue rating considers individual characteristics of the firms bond issues (such as the bonds seniority ranking).

Question #2: Given your knowledge of the agencies “notching” process, a junior subordinated bond from a firm with a BB
issuer rating, would most likely carry a issue rating of:

A: B+   The notching process would mean the individual bond issue would carry a lower rating (the bond is junior subordinated).
B: BB
C: BBB-
D: A
     Credit Ratings – Risks of relying on Agency Ratings

(1) Credit and default risk is extremely dynamic
•    Credit ratings fluctuate over time. Over any given 3-year period, 30% of bond issues change rating category.
•    Ratings almost never remain unchanged from the time of issue to the maturity of the bond.
•    Highlights the risk of the changing nature of credit risk over the bond holding period.


(2) Rating agencies give an opinion on the bond rating (not fact).
•    All rating agencies mis-rated billions of dollars of subprime-backed mortgage securities (extremely toxic debt securities) in the run-up to the
     Great Financial Crisis in 2007/08.
•    Other examples include the mis-rating of Enron (wide scale financial fraud) and WorldCom (went bust during the Dotcom bubble). In both
     cases, the agencies missed egregious financial/accounting fraud.

(3) Market shocks (idiosyncratic risk) is very difficult to capture in ratings
•    Risk such as environmental catastrophe (increasingly relevant today), litigation risk, or some other risk that cannot reasonably be forecasted
     will not be reflected in bond ratings.
•    For example, Tokyo based power utility “Tokyo Electric” had an investment grade rating prior to the impact of the tsunami that hit Japan on
     March 11th 2011.

(4) Ratings lag the market pricing of credit risk [Application 6 – Forecasting Default]
•    Market implied measures of bond default risk (including the bond price, yield and credit spreads) react far quicker to changes or perceived
     changes in the default risk of a firm.
•    Rating agency ratings almost always lag the market pricing of firm default risk.
 Application 6: Predicting aggregate credit spreads and default rates – Excel

• See Application 6 in the Lecture 5 Excel Sheet.
• We define the aggregate rate of bond default, DR, as the number of bonds that are assigned a Default Rating
  (“D”) or extremely likely or already close to default (“C”) rating from the S&P Rating Agency, divided by the
  total number of bonds in each month t. We then compute the monthly change in the default rate to get 𝐷𝐷𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡 .
• Aggregate credit spread index, is the equally-weighted average of all bonds traded in the U.S. OAS (Option
  adjusted Credit Spread). 𝐶𝐶𝑆𝑆𝑡𝑡 is the monthly change in the credit spread index.
• To forecast changes in rating implied default rates and credit spreads, we estimate the following OLS
  regression models:
                                     𝐷𝐷𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡+1 = 𝛼𝛼 + 𝛽𝛽𝑡𝑡𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 𝐶𝐶𝑆𝑆𝑡𝑡 + 𝛽𝛽𝑡𝑡𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷 𝐷𝐷𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝑡𝑡
                                        𝐶𝐶𝑆𝑆𝑡𝑡+1 = 𝛼𝛼 + 𝛽𝛽𝑡𝑡𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 𝐶𝐶𝑆𝑆𝑡𝑡 + 𝛽𝛽𝑡𝑡𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷 𝐷𝐷𝑅𝑅𝑡𝑡 + 𝜀𝜀𝑡𝑡 ,
where, 𝛼𝛼 is the intercept, 𝛽𝛽𝑡𝑡𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 is the coefficient on month t changes in credit spreads and 𝛽𝛽𝑡𝑡𝐷𝐷𝐷𝐷 is the coefficient
on month t changes in rating implied default rates.
• If market based measures of default risk (i.e., Aggregate credit spread index) are effective in predicting default
  risk, we have the following hypothesis:
                                𝐻𝐻0 : 𝛽𝛽𝑡𝑡𝐶𝐶𝐶𝐶 > 0 , with a t-statistic greater than 1.96 (or 2)
 Decomposing Bond Returns – Credit & Interest Rate Components
•   We now want to compute what is known as the corporate bond “excess” return (as it is known in the industry).
•   Excess return is a measure of relative value that neutralizes the interest rate and yield curve risk of a bond, thereby isolating that portion of its
    performance that is attributed solely to credit and optionality risks.
•   Bond excess return, is different to stock excess return, which is generally considered to be the total return of the stock minus the one-month Treasury
    Bill rate of return.
•   With bonds, we need to account for the fact that a large portion of the movement in the bonds price (and hence the return) is due to movements in
    the yield curve.
•   Subtracting the one-month risk-free rate from the total bond return does not account for interest rate risk because it ignores the maturity (or
    duration) of the bond.
•   As we learnt in prior lectures, bonds with greater duration, have higher interest rate risk – the prices of these bonds are more sensitive to changes in
    the yield curve, meaning a greater portion of their return stems from interest rate movement as opposed to changes in the credit risk or
    fundamentals of the issuing firm!
•   The process of computing a bond excess return is very similar to computing a bond credit spread, we can:
              A) Compute the maturity matched U.S. Treasury bond rate of return
              B) Compute the duration matched U.S. Treasury bond rate of return
•   Bond excess return is then defined as:
                                                                      𝐵𝐵𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜      𝐵𝐵𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜      𝐺𝐺
                                                                    𝑅𝑅𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 = 𝑟𝑟𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇  − 𝑟𝑟𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 ,
Where 𝑟𝑟𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇
         𝐺𝐺
              is the maturity or duration matched Treasury Bond rate of return and 𝑅𝑅𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇
                                                                                     𝐵𝐵𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜
                                                                                              is the excess return of bond t for month t.
In practise, bond traders will hedge their interest rate risk exposure a combination of Treasury bond futures and swaps.

  the key-rate U.S. T-Bond returns.

  provider.

  portfolio returns that hedged away the interest rate exposure of the 2-bonds.
                                                                          𝐵𝐵𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜      𝐵𝐵𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜𝑜      𝐺𝐺
• The maturity matched excess returns are then computed every month as: 𝑅𝑅𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 = 𝑟𝑟𝑖𝑖,𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇  − 𝑟𝑟𝑡𝑡,𝑇𝑇 .
      The Single Factor Corporate Bond Market Model – Bond CAPM
• In the bond literature, it is most common to form 5 (quintile) or 10 (decile) portfolios sorted on betas on month t.
Step 1: Estimating individual bond betas
•    Estimate the betas of all the bonds that we will use to create the quintile portfolios. We estimate β1,t for bond 1, β2,t for bond 2, …, βN,t for bond N using
    the return available up to month t but not after month t (no forward-looking information). We estimate the beta using the value-weighted bond market
    return factor.
Step 2: Ranking bonds into 10 portfolios with increasing betas
• Given all bond betas have been computed, we compute the 10 beta-decile breakpoints such that approximately 10% of the bonds are assigned to each
  decile in each month t. The decile breakpoints are dynamic and change every month.
Step 3: Calculating the 10 portfolio returns for month t+1 using information up to month t
• Let’s suppose that bonds 1, 5, and 6 are in Portfolio 1, the lowest beta portfolio (Portfolio 10 is the highest beta portfolio).
• The return of portfolio 1 (P1) on month t+1 is then rP1,t+1 = w1,t r1,t+1 + w5,t r5,t+1 + w6,t r6,t+1 where w1,t is the weight of bond 1 in P1. If we create
  equally-weighted portfolios, w1,t = w5,t = w6,t = 1/3 in this case.
                                                                               1
• The beta of P1 is the lowest of the remaining portfolios because βP1,t = (β1,t + β5,t + β6,t ) and β1,t , β5,t , and β6,t are the lowest betas among all the other
                                                                          3
  bonds by construction (Step 2).
• Do the same for the remaining portfolios for obtaining returns on month t+1 of P2, P3, etc. …
Step 4: Repeat Step 1-3 for all months t+2, t+3, …, T in the sample.
Output: This yields a T x 10 matrix of “out-of-sample” bond portfolio returns, where P10 contains bonds with the highest betas and P1 contains bonds with the
lowest betas.
 The Bond CAPM … “Works” …




  •   Unlike what we observed in Lectures #1 and #2, the relationship between the average bond portfolio returns (in excess of the 1-Month T-Bill in Panel (A) and the
      Excess returns in Panel (B)), line up extremely well with the SML.
  •   The predicted average excess returns from the bond market beta align very well with actual realized average returns.
  •   Except for the very extreme decile (Decile 10), the portfolio betas almost perfectly align with the average monthly bond returns.
  •   Very high beta bonds earn a higher premium than what is predicted by the SML. The remaining portfolios are almost perfectly aligned.
  •   This finding calls into question many empirical papers in the asset pricing literature that have attempted to find ad hoc factors which explain bond returns (they
      were never needed in the first place!).
 Corporate Bond Market Anomalies
 • The relation between beta and the average return (of 10 portfolios sorted on their betas) demonstrates a very good
   fit.
 • The realized returns of the high beta portfolio (Decile 10) is too high than what the model predicts, but this is the only
   point of failure.
 • Despite this, various authors in the field of empirical asset pricing have gone “factor fishing” to generate factors that
   could augment the single-factor bond market.
 • Evidence for additional factors which explain bond expected returns include:
      • Fama & French (1993) shows that a 3-factor model including the stock market factor (CAPM), a default risk
        factor (DEF) and a term structure factor (TERM) is suitable in capturing bond expected returns.

       • Bali, Bai and Wen (2019) or BBW design a 4-factor model which include the bond market factor (MKTbond), the
         default risk factor (DRF), a credit risk factor (CRF) and a liquidity risk factor (LRF). However, the paper is not able
         to be replicated and results to not hold across bond databases. Note that BBW use bond returns in excess of
         the 1-Month T-Bill return, and do not account for interest rate risk.

       • Israel, Palhares and Richardson (2017), build a 4-factor model comprising the Carry, Defensive, Momentum and
         Value factor which explains a large portion of the cross-sectional variation in corporate bond excess returns.

       • Van Binsbergen and Schwert (2022) show that the stock CAPM can explain bond expected returns using bond
         excess returns, i.e., bond returns that have been stripped of interest rate risk.
